briefing.headline: 12 high-priority items in the latest EM scan
briefing.summary: Top sources today: AJEM, EMJ, EMCrit. Main themes: trials, guideline or formal recommendation, reviews. Best early reads: 2025 guideline updates for community-acquired pneumonia diagnosis and management; Acute hyperkalaemia in emergency care: evidence-based approaches.
briefing.editorialHeadline: From Hyperkalemia to CAP: Key Shifts in Acute Care Algorithms
briefing.editorialSummary:
The current literature set demands attention across several high-yield, algorithm-driven areas. For managing acute hyperkalemia, the message remains clear: calcium is for membrane stabilization, not potassium removal, and the preferred initial shift strategy involves insulin-glucose combined with inhaled beta-agonists, while avoiding reliance on older binders like sodium polystyrene sulfonate. In pulmonary medicine, the 2025 CAP guidelines suggest a potential paradigm shift, advising that antibiotics might be unnecessary for otherwise healthy patients with a positive viral screen, and recommending a minimum three-day course for those who do need treatment.

Beyond routine infections, the management of critically ill patients requires synthesizing multiple guidelines. For those crashing with PE, the updated EMCrit framework offers a valuable, consolidated algorithm to harmonize advanced therapies. Similarly, when faced with an undiagnosed poisoning, adopting a systematic toxidromic approach provides a necessary structure for the initial workup. Finally, in pediatric septic shock, the PRoMPT BOLUS trial offers reassuring data suggesting that the choice between 0.9% saline and balanced crystalloids is clinically neutral for initial resuscitation.

These updates—from refining fluid choices in sepsis to refining antibiotic stewardship in CAP—underscore a trend toward evidence-based de-escalation and algorithmic refinement across the spectrum of acute care.

dailyEditorial.title: 2025 guideline updates for community-acquired pneumonia diagnosis and management
dailyEditorial.takeaway: When managing stable CAP, remember the guidelines suggest considering de-escalation or withholding antibiotics entirely if the patient is otherwise healthy and has a positive viral screen. For those who do need antibiotics, aim for a minimum 3-day course for both outpatient and nonsevere inpatient settings. Always confirm institutional protocols regarding the utility of lung ultrasound in your specific ED setting.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: The new 2025 American Thoracic Society guidelines provide an updated framework for diagnosing and managing community-acquired pneumonia (CAP) in the emergency department, addressing areas like imaging, antibiotics, and…
dailyEditorial.summary: The new 2025 American Thoracic Society guidelines provide an updated framework for diagnosing and managing community-acquired pneumonia (CAP) in the emergency department, addressing areas like imaging, antibiotics, and corticosteroids. A key update supporting the role of lung ultrasound by experienced clinicians is highlighted, which should be useful for rapid bedside assessment. Perhaps most notable for practice is the suggestion that antibiotics might be unnecessary for otherwise healthy patients who test positive for a virus. Furthermore, the guidelines advocate for potentially shortening antibiotic courses, recommending a minimum of three days for both stable outpatients and admitted patients with nonsevere CAP.

dailyEditorial.title: Acute hyperkalaemia in emergency care: evidence-based approaches
dailyEditorial.takeaway: When managing acute hyperkalemia, remember that calcium is for cardiac membrane stabilization, not potassium removal. The preferred initial shift strategy is insulin-glucose combined with inhaled beta-agonists. Avoid relying on sodium polystyrene sulfonate, and while newer binders are promising, they lack sufficient acute ED data to change immediate management protocols.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This review provides a comprehensive, evidence-based summary of managing acute hyperkalemia in the ED setting, which is crucial given its potential for life-threatening cardiac arrhythmias.
dailyEditorial.summary: This review provides a comprehensive, evidence-based summary of managing acute hyperkalemia in the ED setting, which is crucial given its potential for life-threatening cardiac arrhythmias. The core principles emphasize immediate stabilization with intravenous calcium salts if ECG changes are present or if potassium levels reach ≥6.5 mmol/L. For definitive lowering, insulin-glucose therapy remains a mainstay, and combining this with inhaled beta-agonists like salbutamol is recommended for synergistic effect. Notably, the review advises against using sodium polystyrene sulfonate due to questionable efficacy and GI risks, while also flagging that newer binders like sodium zirconium cyclosilicate need more acute care validation. Haemodialysis is appropriately reserved for refractory or end-stage renal disease cases.

dailyEditorial.title: EMCrit 424 – 2026 Crashing PE Update
dailyEditorial.takeaway: When managing a crashing PE, use this updated algorithm to harmonize the AHA categories with the EMCrit framework for a clearer pathway. Remember that the decision between thrombolysis, thrombectomy, or ECMO must be highly individualized based on ongoing hemodynamic status and institutional capability. Be mindful that this synthesis is designed to improve workflow, but local protocols must always guide the final treatment plan.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This update synthesizes the latest evidence regarding the management of hemodynamically significant pulmonary embolism (PE), effectively merging the new AHA consensus guidelines with the established EMCrit risk-stratifi…
dailyEditorial.summary: This update synthesizes the latest evidence regarding the management of hemodynamically significant pulmonary embolism (PE), effectively merging the new AHA consensus guidelines with the established EMCrit risk-stratification framework. The core utility here is providing a cohesive, actionable algorithm that covers the spectrum of advanced therapies, including systemic thrombolysis, mechanical thrombectomy, and the use of ECMO. It’s particularly helpful for reconciling the sometimes disparate language between major society guidelines and practical, real-world bedside decision-making. The article synthesizes recent randomized data to refine the therapeutic pathway, offering a more integrated approach for critically ill patients with PE. This consolidation should streamline decision-making when managing the most unstable patients.

dailyEditorial.title: Management of patients with suspected but unidentified poisoning in the emergency department: a joint Royal College of Emergency Medicine and National Poisons Information Service best practice guideline
dailyEditorial.takeaway: When faced with an undiagnosed poisoning, adopt a systematic toxidromic approach, focusing on the expected toxicokinetics of potential agents to guide initial stabilization and workup. Remember this guideline is a general framework, not a substitute for specific poison protocols; always defer to TOXBASE or the local poisons centre for definitive management steps. Keep the focus on stabilizing the patient while methodically narrowing the differential based on clinical presentation.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This new joint guideline from the Royal College of Emergency Medicine and the National Poisons Information Service provides a comprehensive, generalized framework for managing patients in the ED where poisoning is suspe…
dailyEditorial.summary: This new joint guideline from the Royal College of Emergency Medicine and the National Poisons Information Service provides a comprehensive, generalized framework for managing patients in the ED where poisoning is suspected but not yet confirmed. The core concept revolves around adopting a toxidromic approach, meaning the management strategy must be highly attuned to the toxicokinetics of potential agents and anticipate how the patient's clinical status might evolve. It guides the initial assessment process, helping clinicians systematically narrow down the differential diagnosis of ingested toxins. Crucially, the authors stress that this guideline is a general clinical support tool and explicitly does not supersede the specific, detailed poison information found in resources like TOXBASE or the local NPIS. This structured approach is designed to bring consistency to a notoriously complex area of emergency medicine.

dailyEditorial.title: PRoMPT BOLUS: A Landmark PECARN Trial Defining Fluid Choice in Pediatric Sepsis
dailyEditorial.takeaway: When managing pediatric septic shock, the choice between 0.9% saline and balanced crystalloids appears to be clinically neutral based on this trial. You can confidently use either fluid type for initial resuscitation without worrying about a differential impact on major outcomes like kidney injury or mortality. However, remember that this evidence pertains to fluid choice and does not negate the need for timely source control or vasopressor support if hypotension persists.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: The PRoMPT BOLUS trial provides a large, pragmatic randomized comparison of 0.9% saline versus balanced crystalloids for initial fluid resuscitation in pediatric septic shock, addressing a long-standing debate in critic…
dailyEditorial.summary: The PRoMPT BOLUS trial provides a large, pragmatic randomized comparison of 0.9% saline versus balanced crystalloids for initial fluid resuscitation in pediatric septic shock, addressing a long-standing debate in critical care. The study's primary finding is that there was no significant difference between the two fluid types when assessed across several clinically relevant endpoints, including the incidence of kidney injury and overall mortality. This robust evidence suggests that the choice between these two common resuscitation fluids may not impact patient outcomes in the real-world setting. This is a significant piece of data for guiding fluid management protocols in pediatric septic shock.

dailyEditorial.title: Mothership and Drip-and-Ship Strategies in Mechanical Thrombectomy for Acute Ischemic Stroke
dailyEditorial.takeaway: For AIS-LVO, the evidence synthesis suggests that the optimal transfer pathway remains debated, but the analysis provides a quantitative comparison of outcomes between direct transport versus local evaluation first. While the meta-analysis is helpful, remember that institutional protocols and local resource availability must guide decision-making, and the decision should weigh the potential benefits of early advanced care against the risks of unnecessary transfer delays.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This systematic review and meta-analysis tackles the persistent debate surrounding the best out-of-hospital transfer strategy for patients presenting with acute ischemic stroke secondary to a large vessel occlusion (AIS…
dailyEditorial.summary: This systematic review and meta-analysis tackles the persistent debate surrounding the best out-of-hospital transfer strategy for patients presenting with acute ischemic stroke secondary to a large vessel occlusion (AIS-LVO). Specifically, it compares the outcomes of the 'mothership' approach—which involves direct transport to a comprehensive stroke center—against the 'drip-and-ship' model, where initial evaluation occurs at a local facility before transfer. The authors synthesized evidence to determine if one pathway offers a superior clinical benefit regarding patient outcomes. Given the high stakes in managing AIS-LVO, clarifying the optimal referral pathway is crucial for improving time-to-treatment metrics.

dailyEditorial.title: Minor Stroke, Major Uncertainty
dailyEditorial.takeaway: Unless the minor stroke presentation involves a clearly demonstrable and measurable deficit, reserve IVT administration. Don't treat all minor strokes the same; the current evidence suggests that routine use without clear impairment markers is likely unnecessary. Keep in mind that this recommendation emphasizes the need for specific deficit assessment before considering thrombolysis.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This piece tackles the often murky area of intravenous thrombolytics (IVT) use in patients presenting with minor stroke.
dailyEditorial.summary: This piece tackles the often murky area of intravenous thrombolytics (IVT) use in patients presenting with minor stroke. The core message revolves around the need for more targeted research, suggesting that future randomized controlled trials should move beyond simply assessing overall benefit and instead investigate which specific neurological deficits might actually benefit from reperfusion therapy. The authors strongly caution against the routine administration of IVT to anyone presenting with a minor stroke unless there is clear, measurable evidence of significant impairment. Essentially, it argues for a more nuanced, deficit-specific approach rather than blanket application.

dailyEditorial.title: Refining Early Rule-Out Strategies for Acute Myocardial Infarction: Insights and Opportunities From the LEGEND Trial
dailyEditorial.takeaway: Consider implementing a protocol that pairs a single high-sensitivity troponin measurement with structured shared decision-making for low-pretest probability patients. This approach aims to safely accelerate disposition decisions, potentially reducing unnecessary resource utilization. Remember that while promising, this model needs careful local implementation to ensure shared decision-making is robustly applied.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: The LEGEND trial provides an interesting look at streamlining the workup for acute myocardial infarction (AMI) in the emergency department using a combination approach.
dailyEditorial.summary: The LEGEND trial provides an interesting look at streamlining the workup for acute myocardial infarction (AMI) in the emergency department using a combination approach. This stepped-wedge cluster randomized trial tested a model that integrates a single high-sensitivity cardiac troponin measurement with shared decision-making to potentially expedite the rule-out process and shorten hospital stays. The core concept is to move beyond sequential testing by using this combined strategy to make quicker disposition decisions for low-risk patients. This approach is particularly relevant given the ongoing pressure to improve ED throughput without compromising diagnostic accuracy for AMI. The findings suggest a potentially clinically useful pathway for risk stratification in this common, yet complex, presentation.

dailyEditorial.title: LIBERATE-D – Conservative Dialysis Strategy
dailyEditorial.takeaway: For stable AKI-D patients anticipated to need outpatient dialysis, the data suggest that withholding dialysis until clear indications are present might support better kidney function recovery compared to routine thrice-weekly treatments. While this represents a shift away from standard practice, remember that this strategy must be carefully titrated, and the decision to initiate dialysis should remain highly individualized based on clinical status, not just a fixed schedule.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: The LIBERATE-D trial sheds light on the management of stable adults with acute kidney injury-dialysis-requiring (AKI-D) status who are expected to need outpatient dialysis.
dailyEditorial.summary: The LIBERATE-D trial sheds light on the management of stable adults with acute kidney injury-dialysis-requiring (AKI-D) status who are expected to need outpatient dialysis. The core question addressed is whether adopting a conservative, indication-triggered dialysis strategy yields better kidney function recovery compared to the standard of care, which involves routine thrice-weekly dialysis. This is particularly relevant because current guidelines lack robust scientific backing for maintaining rigid, scheduled dialysis regimens in this population. The trial sought to balance the potential benefits of aggressive renal support against the risks associated with unnecessary dialysis treatments in stable patients. Overall, the study provides data to refine the timing and necessity of dialysis initiation in the setting of AKI-D.

dailyEditorial.title: New Guidelines Emphasize Outpatient Treatment of Asymptomatic Hypertension
dailyEditorial.takeaway: When encountering asymptomatic hypertension, prioritize assessing the patient's linkage to primary care over immediate ED intervention. If follow-up is feasible, initiating oral antihypertensives with a clear PCP referral is the preferred strategy. Be cautious about aggressive BP lowering in the ED setting, as this carries inherent risks.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: The updated ACC/AHA and 2025 ACEP guidelines are shifting the paradigm for managing asymptomatic hypertension encountered in the emergency department.
dailyEditorial.summary: The updated ACC/AHA and 2025 ACEP guidelines are shifting the paradigm for managing asymptomatic hypertension encountered in the emergency department. Rather than defaulting to aggressive in-ED management, the new recommendations strongly favor an outpatient approach for initiating antihypertensive therapy. The core message is that the decision to treat should be highly contingent on the patient's established ability to follow up with primary care. This shift acknowledges that rapid blood pressure lowering in the acute setting carries potential risks, making careful risk stratification paramount before starting medication.

dailyEditorial.title: RENOVATE or an Exercise in Statistical Futility? The Question of Flow or Pressure for Acute Hypoxic Respiratory Failure
dailyEditorial.takeaway: When managing acute hypoxic respiratory failure, the RENOVATE data suggests that choosing between HFNO and NIV may not confer a significant advantage in overall patient outcomes. Continue to use your clinical judgment regarding the underlying pathophysiology and patient tolerance, rather than defaulting to one modality over the other based on this trial alone. Be mindful that the interpretation of these results is complex, and the decision remains highly individualized.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: The RENOVATE trial provides a randomized comparison between high-flow nasal oxygen (HFNO) and noninvasive ventilation (NIV) in patients presenting with acute hypoxic respiratory failure.
dailyEditorial.summary: The RENOVATE trial provides a randomized comparison between high-flow nasal oxygen (HFNO) and noninvasive ventilation (NIV) in patients presenting with acute hypoxic respiratory failure. The study's primary focus was to determine if one modality offered superior outcomes compared to the other in this critically ill population. The results, published in JAMA, suggest that the choice between HFNO and NIV may not significantly impact key clinical endpoints, which is a nuanced finding for emergency practice. This trial adds to the growing body of evidence attempting to solidify guidelines for initial respiratory support in the ED setting. It's particularly relevant because both modalities are increasingly utilized, and clinicians often face uncertainty regarding which approach is best for a given patient presentation.

dailyEditorial.title: Alternate Defibrillation Strategies for Refractory Ventricular Fibrillation
dailyEditorial.takeaway: The data suggests that techniques like Double Sequential Defibrillation might be beneficial for reducing VF burden early in OHCA, potentially shifting initial management protocols. However, this analysis is a secondary review of existing trial data, so clinicians should interpret these signals cautiously. For now, the evidence points toward further dedicated studies to confirm if DSED should be adopted as a standard initial defibrillation strategy.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This review delves into the defibrillation data from the DOSE-VF Trial, specifically examining whether the survival benefit observed in Out-of-Hospital Cardiac Arrest (OHCA) patients can be attributed to reducing the du…
dailyEditorial.summary: This review delves into the defibrillation data from the DOSE-VF Trial, specifically examining whether the survival benefit observed in Out-of-Hospital Cardiac Arrest (OHCA) patients can be attributed to reducing the duration of ventricular fibrillation (VF) between shocks. The analysis compared different defibrillation approaches, including Double Sequential Defibrillation (DSED) and Vector Change Defibrillation (VC), against standard protocols. The key finding suggests that both DSED and VC successfully reduced the time spent in VF compared to standard care, with DSED showing the most encouraging signal. This prompts a discussion about whether these techniques represent a shift in initial management rather than just being reserved for refractory rhythms.

dailyEditorial.title: Endovascular Thrombectomy Boosts Outcomes in Medium-Vessel Ischemic Stroke
dailyEditorial.takeaway: For patients with acute middle-vessel occlusion and moderate-to-severe deficits, ensuring endovascular thrombectomy is performed within 24 hours appears beneficial for functional independence. Remember that this signal is from the ORIENTAL-MeVO trial, so while encouraging, it should be integrated with existing guidelines and local resource availability. Don't delay reperfusion based on this data alone, but use it to advocate for timely advanced imaging and thrombectomy pathways.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: The ORIENTAL-MeVO trial provides encouraging data suggesting that early endovascular thrombectomy can improve functional outcomes in patients presenting with acute middle-vessel occlusion strokes who have moderate to se…
dailyEditorial.summary: The ORIENTAL-MeVO trial provides encouraging data suggesting that early endovascular thrombectomy can improve functional outcomes in patients presenting with acute middle-vessel occlusion strokes who have moderate to severe neurological deficits. Specifically, the analysis points toward improved functional independence when this intervention is performed within a 24-hour window from symptom onset. This is a useful piece of information for guiding acute stroke management protocols, particularly in the setting of confirmed middle-vessel occlusion. While the findings are derived from a Chinese cohort, the emphasis on the time window and the type of deficit makes this relevant for our daily practice. It reinforces the value of timely reperfusion strategies for these specific patient groups.

dailyEditorial.title: Comparison of three point-of-care ultrasound techniques to confirm endotracheal tube placement: A randomized clinical trial
dailyEditorial.takeaway: Given the high diagnostic performance across all three methods, any of these ultrasound techniques can serve as a valuable adjunct for confirming ETT placement. However, remember that TUS was reported to offer the shortest confirmation time, which could be beneficial during a time-critical resuscitation. Proceed with the technique you are most comfortable with, but keep the time efficiency of TUS in mind.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This randomized clinical trial directly compared three different point-of-care ultrasound (POCUS) techniques for confirming endotracheal tube (ETT) placement following rapid sequence intubation.
dailyEditorial.summary: This randomized clinical trial directly compared three different point-of-care ultrasound (POCUS) techniques for confirming endotracheal tube (ETT) placement following rapid sequence intubation. The authors found that all three methods—transcranial ultrasound (TUS), suprasternal ultrasound (SSUS), and ultrasound of the trachea—demonstrated high diagnostic accuracy in confirming the tube's location. Of particular note is the finding that TUS provided the quickest confirmation time among the modalities evaluated. This suggests that while all techniques are reliable adjuncts, TUS might offer a significant advantage in terms of speed at the bedside.

dailyEditorial.title: Association between prehospital time and trauma outcomes: A multicenter cohort study from the Pan-Asian Trauma Outcomes Study (PATOS)
dailyEditorial.takeaway: While the data suggests that minimizing time to resuscitation and on-scene treatment is important for improving mortality and function, remember that these time metrics are not entirely independent of patient acuity. Be cautious about interpreting these associations in isolation, as the sickest patients will naturally take longer to manage. Focus on implementing systemic improvements rather than solely penalizing longer times.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This multicenter cohort study from the Pan-Asian Trauma Outcomes Study (PATOS) investigated how various prehospital time metrics relate to subsequent trauma outcomes.
dailyEditorial.summary: This multicenter cohort study from the Pan-Asian Trauma Outcomes Study (PATOS) investigated how various prehospital time metrics relate to subsequent trauma outcomes. The authors found that longer times to resuscitation (RT) and longer times to on-scene treatment (OST) were associated with higher mortality rates. Furthermore, extended OST and time to transport (TT) correlated with poorer functional outcomes. While these associations suggest that optimizing prehospital time is beneficial, the authors caution that these findings might be confounded by the fact that the most severely ill patients inherently require more time and care, which could bias the results.

dailyEditorial.title: Association between initial bandemia degree and short-term mortality among adults with bacteremia in the emergency department
dailyEditorial.takeaway: When managing a bacteremic patient in the ED, keep in mind that a higher initial band percentage appears to be an independent predictor of worse short-term outcomes. While this warrants consideration in risk stratification, do not let this single parameter overshadow the need for aggressive source control and timely cultures. Be cautious, however, as this is an observational finding and its utility needs to be weighed against established risk scores.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This retrospective cohort study investigated whether the initial percentage of band neutrophils in adults presenting to the emergency department with confirmed bacteremia predicts short-term mortality.
dailyEditorial.summary: This retrospective cohort study investigated whether the initial percentage of band neutrophils in adults presenting to the emergency department with confirmed bacteremia predicts short-term mortality. The authors found a statistically significant association, concluding that a higher initial band percentage is independently linked to an increased risk of death within 30 days. This suggests that the degree of left shift, specifically the band count, might be a useful prognostic marker beyond just the diagnosis of bacteremia itself. While the finding is notable, it is important to remember that this is an observational association, and causality cannot be definitively established from the data presented.

dailyEditorial.title: Endovascular resuscitation: an expert practice review
dailyEditorial.takeaway: When considering EVR for refractory shock or arrest, remember that these techniques are multimodal, combining hemorrhage control with mechanical support. While the principles are evolving rapidly, the core takeaway is understanding the stepwise escalation from initial vascular access through aortic balloon occlusion to full ECMO. Always maintain a critical eye on the local institutional protocols and the specific indications for each intervention, as implementation varies widely.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This expert review provides a comprehensive overview of endovascular resuscitation (EVR), grouping together several advanced therapies designed to bridge critically ill patients in shock or cardiac arrest to definitive…
dailyEditorial.summary: This expert review provides a comprehensive overview of endovascular resuscitation (EVR), grouping together several advanced therapies designed to bridge critically ill patients in shock or cardiac arrest to definitive care. The authors emphasize that EVR combines hemorrhage control with mechanical circulatory support, a concept gaining traction despite ongoing debates regarding its optimal use and associated risks. The review structures its discussion around high-acuity scenarios, specifically out-of-hospital cardiac arrest and cases of non-compressible hemorrhage. It details the underlying physiology, technical approaches, and reported efficacy and safety profiles for three escalating interventions: resuscitative endovascular balloon occlusion of the aorta, selective aortic arch perfusion, and extracorporeal cardiopulmonary resuscitation. For emergency physicians, the goal is to solidify understanding of these principles to better advocate for patients who might benefit from these advanced techniques.

dailyEditorial.title: Eye-related emergency calls and prehospital management in region Zealand, Danmark: a register-based cohort study
dailyEditorial.takeaway: When managing an acute eye complaint in the ED, always maintain a high index of suspicion for underlying neurological pathology, especially stroke. Given the pattern seen here, consider that visual field deficits or acute vision loss might be the sole presenting symptom of a central process. This warrants a thorough neurovascular workup even if the initial complaint seems purely ocular.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This register-based cohort study from Region Zealand, Denmark, analyzed a large dataset of emergency calls initially categorized as eye-related issues.
dailyEditorial.summary: This register-based cohort study from Region Zealand, Denmark, analyzed a large dataset of emergency calls initially categorized as eye-related issues. The authors determined that the most common etiologies for these calls were chemical exposures, general vision loss, and direct trauma, generally resulting in relatively brief hospitalizations. However, the most clinically salient finding is the identification of several patients who presented with primary eye complaints but were subsequently diagnosed with a stroke. This suggests a significant diagnostic overlap or misdirection in the initial triage process, pointing toward a need for enhanced recognition of subtle visual stroke symptoms by prehospital personnel, particularly dispatchers.

dailyEditorial.title: Prehospital Use of Lights and Sirens in Stroke Is Associated With Faster Door-to-CT Times but Not Door-to-Thrombolysis or Door-to-Endovascular Therapy Times
dailyEditorial.takeaway: Don't rely on lights and sirens as a primary driver for improving reperfusion times; the evidence shows they don't shorten door-to-IVT or door-to-EVT. Focus instead on optimizing prehospital notification and hospital activation protocols, as these alternative EMS strategies may offer more tangible benefits than the perceived urgency of L&S alone.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This recent analysis examined the relationship between the use of lights and sirens (L&S) during prehospital transport for stroke patients and several key time metrics in acute stroke care.
dailyEditorial.summary: This recent analysis examined the relationship between the use of lights and sirens (L&S) during prehospital transport for stroke patients and several key time metrics in acute stroke care. The authors found a statistically significant association between L&S use and a reduction in door-to-CT times. However, this benefit did not extend to shortening the time to intravenous thrombolysis (IVT) or endovascular therapy (EVT). Furthermore, the study concluded that L&S use did not translate into improved overall discharge outcomes. The overall implication suggests that while the perceived urgency of L&S might expedite initial imaging, the actual time savings for definitive reperfusion therapies or overall patient outcomes are negligible.

dailyEditorial.title: The prevalence of life-threatening diagnoses in emergency department patients with chest pain in a national group
dailyEditorial.takeaway: Remember that while the overall rate of life-threatening causes is around 1 in 18, ACS remains the most common serious diagnosis encountered. Use these prevalence estimates to temper your suspicion when the initial workup is negative, but do not let them diminish your vigilance for PE or pneumothorax. These figures are best used as a general guide for risk stratification, not as a replacement for clinical judgment.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This national study provides an estimate of the true prevalence of life-threatening etiologies among the general population presenting to the emergency department with atraumatic chest pain.
dailyEditorial.summary: This national study provides an estimate of the true prevalence of life-threatening etiologies among the general population presenting to the emergency department with atraumatic chest pain. The authors report that roughly one in every eighteen patients in this cohort will actually have a condition requiring immediate, life-saving intervention. Among these serious diagnoses, Acute Coronary Syndrome emerges as the most frequent, followed by Pulmonary Embolism, pneumothorax, and Tension Pneumothorax. The data suggest that many chest pain presentations are benign, but the established rates allow clinicians to better stratify risk and establish pre-test probabilities before ordering extensive workups. This quantification is useful for guiding diagnostic suspicion in the ED setting.
